title: Vibe Coding in Action: Building an AI App from Scratch Using Cursor and Gemma
author: Brahim Guaali
text:
Vibe Coding in Action: Building an AI App from Scratch Using Cursor and Gemma
A few weeks ago, While trying to experiment with Vibe Coding, I found myself stuck in the middle of a personal project that felt like it would never end. I had a vision, the motivation, and the tech — but I kept running into the same wall: scope creep, shifting ideas, and never quite reaching the “it’s done” moment. I wanted a win. A small, contained, yet meaningful project that I could start and wrap up fast, while learning something new in the process.
Last week, I came across a LinkedIn post from my colleague Csongor Vogel about a Flutter app running Gemma 3 locally on-device using flutter_gemma
by Sasha Denisov. It immediately caught my eye.
Gemma is a fascinating piece of tech — and experimenting with running LLMs offline in Flutter was definitely exciting — but what made it even more appealing was that it gave me the perfect space to explore something I’ve been deeply interested in: vibe coding.
What is Vibe Coding?
Vibe coding is how I describe a more fluid, AI-assisted way of building software. It’s a shift from traditional processes to a development flow that feels more like a conversation — between you, your ideas, and your AI partner. In this approach:
- AI is your co-pilot, helping with architecture, implementation, and debugging
- Prompting is a craft — the better you express your intent, the better your results
- You stay in flow, moving fast between product, code, and iteration
- Cursor becomes your full-stack ideation + coding environment
Vibe PMing: Starting with Product Clarity
Before I jumped into code, I used Cursor to do something I now call Vibe PMing. I treated AI like a product collaborator, working through the purpose of the app, what features mattered most, and how to slice it into something I could actually ship.
This resulted in a focused Product Definition Requirements (PDR) doc, which gave me the clarity I needed to move forward with intention — not just vibes. That document kept me anchored as I started building.
Architecture: Planning with AI
With the PDR in hand, I moved to designing the system.
- Clean Architecture as the backbone
- BLoC for state management
- Separation between
core
,features
, andshared
layers - Well-scoped responsibilities across modules
I also documented this into a Technical Architecture Guide, keeping the project organized and scalable.
Implementation: Building GenLite with AI
With product goals and architecture in place, I dove into building. Every step was a tight loop between me and AI in Cursor:
Model Management
AI helped me implement:
- Resumable model downloads (~4GB)
- Progress tracking
- Error handling and state persistence
Chat Interface
I wanted real-time, streaming responses from the local model. AI helped me implement a token-based callback system that rendered messages live.
File Analysis
I added support for uploading and querying files (PDF, TXT, DOCX) — all processed locally on-device.
Debugging Token Handling
When I hit 401 errors from Hugging Face, AI helped me debug the .env
setup and pass tokens correctly.
UI Design System
Instead of styling ad-hoc, I prompted AI to help create a small Material 3-based design system for consistent, accessible UI components.
Each feature was scoped, prompted, and implemented collaboratively.
Why It Matters
This project wasn’t just about the app — it was about how it was built.
🧠 Vibe Coding Speeds Up MVPs
AI helped me move from idea to working prototype fast, without skipping on architecture or maintainability.
🧭 Vibe PMing Anchors the Build
Having a solid PDR upfront made everything smoother. It kept the scope tight and the direction clear.
🔄 Short Loops, Better Decisions
Whether I was debugging file paths or designing chat UX, I stayed in tight feedback loops that kept development light and creative.
The Result: GenLite
What I ended up with is GenLite — a cross-platform offline AI assistant built in Flutter with:
- ✅ Offline Chat with Gemma 2B
- ✅ File Upload and Analysis
- ✅ Streaming Responses
- ✅ Resumable Model Downloads
- ✅ Modular, Scalable Codebase
- ✅ Privacy-First Approach: No cloud dependency
Final Thoughts
Experimenting with vibe coding has been one of the most refreshing development experiences I’ve had in a while. It blends architecture, implementation, and product thinking in a way that feels natural — and honestly, fun.
That said, I’m not saying vibe coding is perfect. Like any approach, it comes with its own challenges:
Challenges I Encountered
- Prompt quality is everything — unclear prompts = bad results
- AI hallucination — confident output that’s just wrong or made up
- Superficial understanding — easy to move on without grasping what the code really does
- Debugging AI code takes time — looks good but behaves weirdly
- Inconsistent patterns — different styles or structures across features if not guided well
- Silent technical debt — quick wins can pile up into long-term mess
- Over-reliance on AI — risks weakening your own design and problem-solving instincts
- AI doesn’t push back — it builds what you ask, even if it’s not the best solution
- Version drift — regenerating older code can create inconsistencies
- Lack of high-level context — AI thinks in isolated chunks, not across the whole product vision
Despite all that, for MVPs and fast prototyping, vibe coding is incredibly effective. It helped me move fast, stay in flow, and actually ship something solid.
One of the tools I’m planning to experiment with next is Cursor Rules — a way to guide the AI toward consistent, opinionated output across a codebase. Once I’ve played with it enough, I’ll share my findings.
If you’re feeling stuck, or if you just want to try a more fluid way to build, here’s my suggestion:
- Start with Vibe PMing
- Use AI to plan, not just code
- Keep your scope tight
- Stay in flow
- And vibe your way to done
References:
- Using Gemma for Flutter apps
- Gemma 2B from Google
- Cursor
- Vibe Coding 101: Pairing with AI to Build Faster
- Clean Architecture in Flutter
